front: What is the core three-component structure of a Generalized Linear Model (GLM)?
back:
1. A random component: The response variable Y belongs to an exponential family distribution (e.g., Normal, Binomial, Poisson).
2. A systematic component: A linear predictor, η = Xβ.
3. A link function, g(·): Connects the mean of the response, E(Y) = μ, to the linear predictor: g(μ) = η.

front: What is the role of the link function in a GLM?
back: The link function, g(μ), transforms the expected value of the response variable (μ) so that it can be modeled as a linear combination of the predictors (Xβ). It connects the non-linear mean to the linear predictor, allowing the model to handle non-Normal response data.

front: Provide an example of a canonical link function for three common distributions: Normal, Binomial, and Poisson.
back:
• Normal (Gaussian): Identity link (g(μ) = μ)
• Binomial: Logit link (g(μ) = log(μ / (1 - μ)))
• Poisson: Log link (g(μ) = log(μ))

front: How are parameters estimated in a GLM, and why is this method used instead of ordinary least squares?
back: Parameters are estimated using Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) to maximize the log-likelihood (Maximum Likelihood Estimation). OLS cannot be used directly because the relationship between the response and predictors is not linear; the link function and non-Normal error structure require a more general iterative approach.

front: Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression is a special case of a GLM. What are its specific components?
back:
• Random Component: Y follows a Normal distribution.
• Systematic Component: Linear predictor η = Xβ.
• Link Function: The identity link (g(μ) = μ).
